purpose: Provides a quantitative framework for comparing supply response capacity across commodities, enabling commodity-specific rather than complex-wide supercycle assessment
components: Shale oil: elasticity ~0.5-1.0, response time months
Lithium: elasticity ~0.3-0.5, response time 2-4 years (brine/chemical processing)
Copper: elasticity ~0.05, response time 12-18 years (hard-rock mining, declining grades)
Greenfield vs. brownfield distinction: brownfield elasticity 0.15-0.25 vs. greenfield that has structurally declined
Blended elasticity: declined from ~0.12 to ~0.08, meaningful but not a regime change
current_assessment: Copper's extremely low supply elasticity makes it the only commodity where structural tightness can persist across multiple business cycles. Shale oil elasticity prevents oil from participating in supercycles. Agricultural elasticity is moderate but biologically constrained.
limitations: Elasticity estimates are point-in-time and may shift with technology
Does not account for demand elasticity or substitution effects
Brownfield vs. greenfield split may shift as existing deposits deplete
Limited data on newer commodities (lithium, cobalt, rare earths)
